title: Butter Bridge message hash collision historical bridge incident

historical_exploit.attack_summary: The Butter Bridge V3.1 (part of MAP Protocol and Butter Network) was exploited. An attacker used a vulnerability in the OmniServiceProxy contract’s retry message verification logic, specifically an abi.encodePacked hash collision with dynamic-bytes fields. This allowed forging a cross-chain retry message that bypassed authentication, resulting in the minting of approximately 1 quadrillion (10^15) MAPO tokens (about 4.8 million times the legitimate ~208 million circulating supply). The attacker dumped ~1 billion fake MAPO into the Uniswap V4 ETH/MAPO pool, extracting roughly $180,000 in liquidity (≈52.21 ETH). The teams immediately paused the bridge and related swaps. User funds in pending swaps are safe, and a patch/audit/redeployment is in progress. The remaining ~999 trillion fake tokens stay in the attacker’s wallet, posing ongoing dilution risk.
historical_exploit.attacker_profile: External attacker or unprivileged caller as described by the SlowMist incident row; exact actor permissions must be re-verified before reporting.
preconditions: A bridge, cross-chain router, gateway, wrapped-asset minter, liquidity pool, or validator/proof path accepts attacker-controlled message, proof, signature, transaction, deposit, configuration, or accounting input.
The accepted input releases existing bridge-held value, mints unbacked wrapped assets, miscredits a deposit, or lets a caller move assets beyond entitlement.
The target mapping must be reachable without relying on privileged key compromise, DNS/frontend phishing, social-account compromise, or pure rug-pull behavior.

value_path.asset_or_reward: bridge-held assets, wrapped assets, bridge liquidity, withdrawals, refunds, or backed cross-chain liabilities
value_path.how_value_was_extracted: The Butter Bridge V3.1 (part of MAP Protocol and Butter Network) was exploited. An attacker used a vulnerability in the OmniServiceProxy contract’s retry message verification logic, specifically an abi.encodePacked hash collision with dynamic-bytes fields. This allowed forging a cross-chain retry message that bypassed authentication, resulting in the minting of approximately 1 quadrillion (10^15) MAPO tokens (about 4.8 million times the legitimate ~208 million circulating supply). The attacker dumped ~1 billion fake MAPO into the Uniswap V4 ETH/MAPO pool, extracting roughly $180,000 in liquidity (≈52.21 ETH). The teams immediately paused the bridge and related swaps. User funds in pending swaps are safe, and a patch/audit/redeployment is in progress. The remaining ~999 trillion fake tokens stay in the attacker’s wallet, posing ongoing dilution risk.
value_path.why_it_paid: The historical row describes real loss, attempted bridge theft, unbacked minting, unauthorized withdrawal, or bridge-accounting failure that can be useful only if BridgeIndex has a matching current live-value extraction path.

mapping.why_this_might_map: BridgeIndex exposes bridge deposits, bot-gas accounting, withdrawal creation/approval/execution, refunds, token/resource mappings, relayer voting, and proposal execution. This historical primitive is useful only if it maps to extracting current BNB/USDT ledger value or backed bridge liabilities from the deployed BridgeIndex system.

notes: SlowMist attack method: Smart Contract Vulnerability.
Selected for scanned because: retry message hash collision allowed forged cross-chain message
This seed is not proof that BridgeIndex is vulnerable; Workflow 7 must reject it unless the primitive maps to non-admin extraction of a current live ledger row.
